edges.description: Mislocalization of the PTS2 enzymes dihydroxyacetonephosphate acyltransferase and alkyldihydroxyacetonephosphate synthase blocks the peroxisomal steps of ether-phospholipid synthesis, depleting plasmalogens.
Loss of imported phytanoyl-CoA hydroxylase impairs peroxisomal phytanic acid alpha-oxidation, allowing dietary phytanic acid to accumulate progressively in plasma and tissues.
Plasmalogen deficiency disrupts cartilage and bone development, producing rhizomelic shortening and chondrodysplasia punctata.
Plasmalogen deficiency disrupts lens fiber development, producing congenital cataracts.
Plasmalogen deficiency disrupts CNS neuronal and myelin development, producing intellectual disability and seizures.
The skeletal dysplasia produces proximal limb shortening, especially of the humerus.
Abnormal cartilage and epiphyseal development produces punctate calcifications and epiphyseal stippling.
Vertebral-body dysplasia produces characteristic coronal clefts.
Severe developmental skeletal disease is accompanied by profound postnatal growth deficiency.
Musculoskeletal involvement includes multiple joint contractures, especially in nonclassic disease.
Lens fiber developmental disruption produces congenital or early-infantile cataracts.
CNS developmental impairment produces severe intellectual disability in classic RCDP1.
CNS involvement in classic RCDP1 commonly includes seizures.
Decreased plasmalogens report failure of peroxisomal ether-lipid synthesis.
Elevated phytanic acid reports impaired peroxisomal alpha-oxidation.

nodes.description: Cataracts are usually present at birth or appear in the first months of life and occur in both classic and nonclassic forms.

evidence.snippet: cataracts that are usually present at birth or appear in the first few months of life

nodes.description: Surgical removal of cataracts can restore and/or preserve vision.

evidence.snippet: cataract extraction may restore and/or preserve vision

nodes.description: Punctate calcifications in cartilage with epiphyseal stippling are the radiographic hallmark, giving the disorder its name.

evidence.snippet: punctate calcifications in cartilage with epiphyseal and metaphyseal abnormalities (chondrodysplasia punctata, or CDP)

nodes.description: Plasmalogen deficiency disrupts lens fiber development, producing cataracts usually present at birth or in the first months of life.

evidence.snippet: cataracts that are usually present at birth or appear in the first few months of life

nodes.description: Coronal clefts of the vertebral bodies are a characteristic spinal radiographic finding in classic RCDP1.

evidence.snippet: coronal clefts of the vertebral bodies

nodes.description: Dietary restriction of phytanic acid may benefit individuals with mild (nonclassic) RCDP1 by limiting progressive phytanic acid accumulation.

evidence.snippet: Dietary restriction of phytanic acid to avoid the consequences of phytanic acid accumulation over time may benefit individuals with mild PEX7-RCDP.

nodes.description: Intellectual disability is severe in classic RCDP1 and milder in the nonclassic form.

evidence.snippet: Intellectual disability is severe, and most children develop seizures.

nodes.description: Multiple joint contractures occur, and are a notable feature of the nonclassic (mild) form.

evidence.snippet: joint contractures, neurobehavioral abnormalities, and milder intellectual disability

nodes.description: CNS plasmalogen deficiency impairs neuronal and myelin development, producing severe intellectual disability and seizures.

evidence.snippet: Intellectual disability is severe, and most children develop seizures.

meta.association: Biallelic loss-of-function variants in PEX7 cause RCDP1 (PBD complementation group 11). Disease severity correlates with residual PEX7 allele activity; the recurrent L292ter null allele is associated with the severe phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Mutational spectrum in the PEX7 gene and functional analysis of mutant alleles in 78 patients with rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 1.
evidence.snippet: we show by functional analysis that disease severity correlates with PEX7 allele activity

evidence.reference_title: Human PEX7 encodes the peroxisomal PTS2 receptor and is responsible for rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata.
evidence.snippet: we found two inactivating PEX7 mutations: one, L292ter, was present in 26 of the probands, all with a severe phenotype

nodes.description: Defective peroxisomal alpha-oxidation allows phytanic acid, derived from dietary sources, to accumulate over time. Accumulation is progressive and diet-dependent, contributing to disease in the milder, longer-surviving nonclassic form and providing the rationale for dietary phytanic acid restriction.

evidence.snippet: Dietary restriction of phytanic acid to avoid the consequences of phytanic acid accumulation over time may benefit individuals with mild PEX7-RCDP.
Dietary restriction of phytanic acid to avoid the consequences of phytanic acid accumulation over time may benefit individuals with mild PEX7-RCDP.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated phytanic acid reports impaired peroxisomal alpha-oxidation.

nodes.description: Plasmalogens are ether glycerophospholipids whose peroxisomal synthesis depends on the PTS2 enzymes lost in RCDP1. Their deficiency disrupts membrane and myelin composition and developmental signaling in bone, brain, lens, and other tissues, accounting for the skeletal, ocular, and neurologic manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Functions of plasmalogen lipids in health and disease.
evidence.snippet: The RCDP phenotype predicts developmental roles for plasmalogens in bone, brain, lens, lung, kidney and heart.

evidence.reference_title: Quantitative analysis of ethanolamine plasmalogen species in red blood cells using liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry for diagnosing peroxisome biogenesis disorders.
evidence.snippet: Markedly reduced plasmalogens are a classic feature of peroxisome biogenesis disorders (PBD) because plasmalogen synthesis requires functional peroxisomes.

readouts.interpretation: Decreased plasmalogens report failure of peroxisomal ether-lipid synthesis.

nodes.description: Postnatal growth deficiency is profound in classic RCDP1 and milder in the nonclassic form.

evidence.snippet: postnatal growth deficiency is profound.

nodes.description: Symmetrical proximal shortening of the limbs, most marked in the humerus and to a lesser degree the femur, is the defining skeletal feature of RCDP.

evidence.snippet: proximal shortening of the humerus (rhizomelia) and to a lesser degree the femur

nodes.description: Most children with classic RCDP1 develop seizures.

evidence.snippet: Intellectual disability is severe, and most children develop seizures.

nodes.description: Loss of functional peroxin 7 (PEX7), the cytosolic PTS2 receptor, prevents import of the subset of peroxisomal matrix enzymes that carry the type-2 targeting signal. These enzymes are mislocalized to the cytosol and rendered non-functional, while PTS1-dependent import (mediated by PEX5) — including very-long-chain fatty acid beta-oxidation — remains largely intact. This selective import lesion distinguishes RCDP1 from the generalized matrix-import failure of the Zellweger spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: Human PEX7 encodes the peroxisomal PTS2 receptor and is responsible for rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata.
evidence.snippet: PEX7, a candidate gene for RCDP identified in yeast, encodes the receptor for peroxisomal matrix proteins with the type-2 peroxisome targeting signal (PTS2).

evidence.reference_title: Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata is a peroxisomal protein targeting disease caused by a non-functional PTS2 receptor.
evidence.snippet: Like S. cerevisiae pex cells, RCDP cells from CG11 cannot import a PTS2 reporter protein.

evidence.reference_title: Mutational spectrum in the PEX7 gene and functional analysis of mutant alleles in 78 patients with rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 1.
evidence.snippet: the cytosolic PTS2-receptor protein required for targeting a subset of enzymes to peroxisomes. These enzymes are deficient in cells of patients with RCDP, because of their mislocalization to the cytoplasm.

nodes.description: Plasmalogen-dependent disruption of cartilage and bone development produces rhizomelic (proximal) limb shortening, chondrodysplasia punctata with stippled epiphyses, and coronal vertebral clefts.

evidence.snippet: Classic (severe) PEX7-RCDP is characterized by proximal shortening of the humerus (rhizomelia) and to a lesser degree the femur, punctate calcifications in cartilage with epiphyseal and metaphyseal abnormalities (chondrodysplasia punctata, or CDP), coronal clefts of the vertebral bodies, and cataracts that are usually present at birth or appear in the first few months of life.

nodes.description: Management is supportive: physical therapy and orthopedic procedures for contractures, and cataract extraction to preserve vision.

evidence.snippet: Physical therapy to improve contractures; orthopedic procedures may improve function in some individuals
